failed.title: Notes interaction between perpetual wind-down license and source code escrow
failed.match: PASS if the memo notes the interaction between the perpetual post-termination wind-down license and the source code escrow provisions, explaining that together they could allow KBI to terminate the relationship while retaining indefinite technology access. FAIL if this interaction is not identified.

turns.text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

## Context management (automatic)

To keep your context small and your runs cheap, the harness automatically
elides the *raw text* of large `read`/`bash` outputs after they have been on
screen for a few turns, replacing each with a short `[context-saver: …]` stub
that names the file or command. The document was fully read when it was
current — nothing was hidden from your initial pass — only the bulk is dropped
from later turns. Two habits keep this lossless:

- As you read a docum

**NON-BINDING TERM SHEET --- TECHNOLOGY LICENSE AGREEMENT**

**PredictIQ Platform License and Related Services**

May 29, 2025

**CONFIDENTIAL**

Prepared by Bergström Hecht Rechtsanwälte, Maximilianstraße 42, 80538 Munich, Germany, on behalf of Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH. Lead Partner: Dr. Luisa Trautmann.

This term sheet is intended to summarize the principal terms of a proposed technology license arrangement between Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH and Whitmore Analytics Inc. This term sheet is non-binding and does not constitute an offer or agreement. The parties intend to negotiate and execute a definitive agreement based on these terms.

**Targeted Definitive Agreement Execution:** August 15, 2025

**Requested Response Deadline:** KBI respectfully requests that Whitmore provide a written response to this term sheet on or before **June 23, 2025**.

**[Section 1: Transaction Overview]{.underline}**

**Licensor:** Whitmore Analytics Inc., a Delaware corporation (\"**Whitmore**\"), with principal offices at 2400 Westlake Avenue North, Suite 600, Seattle, WA 98109. CEO: Rajesh Malhotra. General Counsel: Sarah Yin.

**Licensee:** Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH, a Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung organized under the laws of Germany, registered in Munich (HRB 247891) (\"**KBI**\"), with principal offices at Leopoldstraße 155, 80804 Munich, Germany. CTO: Dr. Markus Fiedler. VP of Digital Transformation: Annika Solberg.

**Licensee\'s Counsel:** Bergström Hecht Rechtsanwälte, Maximilianstraße 42, 80538 Munich, Germany. Lead Partner: Dr. Luisa Trautmann.

**Licensed Technology:** Whitmore\'s proprietary PredictIQ platform, a cloud-native SaaS platform combining IoT sensor integration with proprietary machine-learning algorithms to predict equipment failures in heavy manufacturing environments, as more fully described in **Exhibit A** attached hereto.

**Purpose:** KBI seeks to integrate PredictIQ into its \"SmartFactory 2030\" initiative to reduce unplanned downtime by 40% across its European manufacturing operations.

**Transaction Structure:** The proposed transaction contemplates: (i) a perpetual, non-exclusive technology license for the PredictIQ platform; (ii) an initial 18-month implementation phase followed by ongoing production use; (iii) a SaaS subscription component for the cloud-hosted analytics engine; and (iv) an on-premises deployment license for edge-computing modules installed at KBI factory sites.

**Deployment Scope:** KBI shall have the right to deploy PredictIQ at up to 43 manufacturing facilities across 12 European countries. KBI shall have the right to expand deployment to any newly acquired manufacturing facilities under the same commercial terms for a period of 5 years following execution of the definitive agreement.

**Key Milestones:**

> • Phase 1 implementation start: September 15, 2025
>
> • Phase 1 completion target: March 15, 2026 (6 months)
>
> • Phase 2 go-live target: March 15, 2027 (18 months from implementation start)

**Whitmore Background:** Whitmore holds 14 issued U.S. utility patents and 3 pending PCT applications related to PredictIQ\'s core algorithms.

**KBI Background:** KBI is a global industrial conglomerate with €8.2B annual revenue, operating 43 manufacturing facilities across 12 European countries.

**[Section 2: License Grant]{.underline}**

**Grant.** Whitmore hereby grants to KBI a perpetual, non-exclusive, worldwide license to use, execute, display, and operate the PredictIQ platform (including all software, algorithms, documentation, APIs, and edge-computing modules) in connection with KBI\'s manufacturing operations.

**Scope.** The license covers both the cloud-hosted SaaS analytics engine and the on-premises edge-computing modules deployed at KBI\'s manufacturing facilities.

**Authorized Users.** KBI employees, contractors, and agents performing work at or for KBI\'s manufacturing facilities shall be authorized to access and use the PredictIQ platform, subject to confidentiality obligations no less restrictive than those set forth in the definitive agreement.

**Sublicensing.** KBI shall not sublicense the PredictIQ platform to third parties, except that KBI may permit its controlled affiliates and subcontractors performing services at KBI facilities to access and use PredictIQ in support of KBI\'s operations.

**Derivative Works.** KBI shall have the right to create derivative works based on PredictIQ\'s application programming interface (API) layer for the sole purpose of integrating the PredictIQ platform with KBI\'s proprietary manufacturing execution systems (MES), supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) systems, and other internal systems. Any and all derivative works created by or on behalf of KBI pursuant to this Section shall be jointly owned by KBI and Whitmore, with each party having the unrestricted right to use, modify, sublicense, and otherwise exploit such derivative works without the consent of, or any obligation to account to, the other party.

**Expansion Right.** For a period of 5 years following execution of the definitive agreement, KBI shall have the right to deploy PredictIQ at any manufacturing facility acquired by KBI after the date of execution, at the same per-facility commercial terms as set forth herein.

**Restrictions.** KBI shall not: (i) reverse engineer, decompile, or disassemble any portion of PredictIQ, except as expressly permitted by applicable law; (ii) use PredictIQ for the benefit of any third party other than as expressly permitted herein; or (iii) remove or alter any proprietary notices on the PredictIQ platform.

**[Section 3: Intellectual Property Ownership]{.underline}**

**3.1 Whitmore IP.** As between the parties, Whitmore retains all right, title, and interest in and to the PredictIQ platform, including all software, algorithms, machine-learning models, source code, object code, documentation, patents (14 issued U.S. utility patents and 3 pending PCT applications), trad

**KESSLER-BRANDT INDUSTRIAL GmbH**

Leopoldstraße 155 · 80804 Munich · Germany Handelsregister: HRB 247891 (Amtsgericht München)

*Office of the Vice President, Digital Transformation*

May 29, 2025

Sarah Yin General Counsel Whitmore Analytics Inc. 2400 Westlake Avenue North, Suite 600 Seattle, WA 98109

**Re: Most-Favored-Customer Pricing Terms --- Proposed Technology License Agreement between Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH and Whitmore Analytics Inc. (the \"PredictIQ License\")**

Dear Ms. Yin:

**[1. Introduction and Context]{.underline}**

This side letter is supplemental to, and is intended to be incorporated into, the definitive Technology License Agreement to be entered into between Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH (\"KBI\") and Whitmore Analytics Inc. (\"Whitmore\") with respect to the PredictIQ platform. Reference is hereby made to the non-binding term sheet dated May 29, 2025, prepared by KBI\'s counsel, Bergström Hecht Rechtsanwälte (Dr. Luisa Trautmann), setting forth the proposed commercial and legal terms for KBI\'s license of the PredictIQ platform (the \"Term Sheet\"). KBI\'s willingness to proceed with the transaction on the commercial terms outlined in the Term Sheet is **expressly conditioned** upon Whitmore\'s acceptance of the most-favored-customer pricing provision set forth in Section 2 below.

KBI has committed to a significant financial investment in this engagement: an Initial License Fee of \$4,200,000, an Annual SaaS Subscription Fee of \$2,850,000 per year, an Implementation Services Fee of \$1,750,000, and an Annual Maintenance Fee of \$756,000 --- representing total first-year revenue to Whitmore of approximately \$9,556,000. Under KBI\'s SmartFactory 2030 initiative, the PredictIQ platform is planned for deployment across 43 manufacturing facilities in 12 European countries. Given the scale and strategic importance of this engagement, KBI considers pricing parity assurance to be a fundamental commercial term.

**[2. Most-Favored-Customer Pricing Provision]{.underline}**

**(a) MFC Commitment.** If at any time during the term of the Agreement (or thereafter, for so long as KBI retains any license rights under the Agreement, including any post-termination wind-down license), Whitmore enters into any agreement, amendment, or arrangement with any third party (a \"Subsequent Licensee\") for the license, subscription, or use of the PredictIQ platform or any successor, derivative, or substantially similar technology, on terms that result in a lower effective per-facility price than the effective per-facility price applicable to KBI under the Agreement, Whitmore shall promptly (and in any event within thirty (30) days of execution of such agreement) notify KBI in writing and retroactively adjust KBI\'s pricing to match the more favorable effective per-facility pricing, effective as of the date such more favorable terms were first offered to the Subsequent Licensee.

**(b) Effective Per-Facility Price Calculation.** \"Effective per-facility price\" shall mean the total annual fees payable by the applicable licensee (including, without limitation, license fees, subscription fees, maintenance and support fees, and implementation or professional services fees, whether characterized as one-time or recurring), divided by the number of facilities at which the PredictIQ platform is deployed or authorized for deployment.

**(c) Retroactive Adjustment and Credit.** Any reduction in KBI\'s effective per-facility price shall be applied retroactively to the date on which Whitmore first offered or agreed to the more favorable terms with the Subsequent Licensee. Whitmore shall issue a credit to KBI equal to the aggregate overpayment for the period from such retroactive date through the date of adjustment, such credit to be applied against KBI\'s next payment(s) due under the Agreement.

**(d) Audit Right.** KBI shall have the right, upon reasonable notice and no more than once per calendar year, to engage an independent auditor (at KBI\'s expense, unless the audit reveals a discrepancy of five percent (5%) or more, in which case at Whitmore\'s expense) to verify Whitmore\'s compliance with this provision. Whitmore shall maintain complete and accurate records of all PredictIQ licensing arrangements sufficient to enable such verification.

**[3. Rationale and Importance to KBI]{.underline}**

As an early and significant adopter of PredictIQ --- committing to deployment across its entire European manufacturing footprint --- KBI expects pricing that reflects the value of this anchor-customer relationship. The effective per-facility pricing metric ensures fair comparison across licensees of different scale. Most-favored-customer protections of this nature are standard commercial practice for transactions of this magnitude; KBI, with annual revenue of €8.2 billion and a position as a leading European industrial conglomerate, routinely includes such provisions in its strategic technology engagements. KBI believes this provision aligns the parties\' long-term commercial interests and fosters an enduring partnership.

**[4. Incorporation into Definitive Agreement]{.underline}**

KBI expects the most-favored-customer pricing provision set forth in Section 2 above to be incorporated verbatim (or in substantially similar form) into the definitive Technology License Agreement targeted for execution on or about August 15, 2025. KBI respectfully requests Whitmore\'s written response to this side letter no later than **June 23, 2025**. KBI\'s willingness to adhere to the proposed transaction timeline --- including the September 15, 2025 Phase 1 implementation start date --- is contingent upon Whitmore\'s acceptance of this provision by such date. KBI acknowledges that this side letter is non-binding in itself but constitutes a material element of KBI\'s overall commercial proposal.

**[5. Confidentiality]{.underline}**

This side letter and its contents are confidential and subject to the confid

From: Tom Kendrick <tkendrick@whitmoreanalytics.com>
To: Sarah Yin <syin@whitmoreanalytics.com>
Cc: Rajesh Malhotra <rmalhotra@whitmoreanalytics.com>
Date: Tue, 03 Jun 2025 08:47:00 -0000
Subject: KBI Term Sheet — Initial Reactions and Concerns
Content-Type: text/plain; charset="utf-8"
Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable
MIME-Version: 1.0

Sarah,

I finished my first full read of the KBI term sheet last night and wanted to =
get my initial reactions to you and Raj while they're fresh. As you know, we =
received the term sheet yesterday (June 2) from Annika Solberg's team =E2=80=
=94 it's dated May 29, 2025, and was prepared by KBI's outside counsel, Bergs=
tr=C3=B6m Hecht Rechtsanw=C3=A4lte (Dr. Luisa Trautmann is the lead partner).=
 The documents =E2=80=94 kbi-whitmore-term-sheet.docx and the kbi-mfc-side-le=
tter.docx =E2=80=94 were circulated to the three of us on Monday, so I'm assu=
ming you've had a chance to at least skim them.

Bottom line up front: I'm excited about this deal. Very excited. But I have s=
erious concerns about three provisions that could do real damage to Whitmore'=
s business if we accept them as-is.

**Why This Deal Matters**

Let me frame the commercial significance first. This would be Whitmore's larg=
est single licensing deal =E2=80=94 by a wide margin. I've run the numbers on=
 first-year revenue:

- License fees: $4,200,000
- SaaS recurring: $2,850,000
- Implementation services: $1,750,000
- Maintenance: $756,000
- **Total first-year: ~$9,600,000**

Against our FY 2024 revenue of $47.3M, that's a flagship deal =E2=80=94 rough=
ly 20% of last year's total. KBI's SmartFactory 2030 initiative is targeting =
43 facilities across 12 European countries with a goal of 40% reduction in un=
planned downtime. Landing KBI as a PredictIQ customer would be the single mos=
t powerful proof point we could have in European heavy manufacturing. This is=
 the reference account that opens the continent.

And the timing matters. Hollcroft Ventures has been asking pointed questions=
 about our path to a Series C round. A deal of this magnitude =E2=80=94 espec=
ially the recurring SaaS component at $2.85M/year =E2=80=94 would materially =
strengthen our growth story. I spoke informally with Hollcroft Point's board obser=
ver last week, and they're "very interested" in the KBI opportunity's contrib=
ution to our revenue trajectory. This deal could be the catalyst for a Series=
 C process later this year or early next.

All of that said, here are my concerns:

**1. Exclusivity / Non-Compete =E2=80=94 This Is My Biggest Issue**

The 3-year exclusivity provision is, in my view, the single most dangerous te=
rm in the sheet. It's not just the 12 named companies on Exhibit B =E2=80=94 =
which themselves include some significant prospects. It's the open-ended defi=
nition that sweeps in *any entity* deriving more than 30% of annual revenue f=
rom automotive or heavy machinery manufacturing in Europe.

I pulled together a quick analysis this morning, and I believe that 30% reven=
ue threshold could capture at least 25 to 30 additional companies beyond the =
12 named ones. Many of these are active prospects in our European pipeline. S=
arah, we currently have late-stage discussions with 3 European automotive/hea=
vy machinery manufacturers for PredictIQ deployments. I won't name them here,=
 but combined potential annual contract value across those 3 is in the range =
of $4-5M in recurring SaaS revenue.

Think about that: our total annual revenue is $47.3M. We'd be locking ourselv=
es out of a significant portion of the European industrial market for three f=
ull years in exchange for one deal =E2=80=94 a big deal, yes, but one that co=
uld actively stunt our growth trajectory and damage the Series C valuation na=
rrative we're trying to build.

My take: the named-company list in Exhibit B might be something we can work w=
ith, though I'd want to negotiate it down. But the open-ended 30% revenue thr=
eshold definition is a non-starter from a go-to-market perspective. I need yo=
u to push hard on narrowing this.

**2. Data Training Restriction =E2=80=94 A Slow-Moving Threat to PredictIQ's =
Core Value**

The second major concern is the clause prohibiting Whitmore from using KBI's =
operational data =E2=80=94 even in anonymized or aggregated form =E2=80=94 to=
 train or improve PredictIQ's general ML models without KBI's prior written c=
onsent.

I want to make sure the strategic significance of this is clear. PredictIQ's =
competitive advantage comes from our ML models continuously improving through=
 exposure to diverse, real-world operational data sets. KBI would be our larg=
est deployment by facility count =E2=80=94 43 facilities generating massive v=
olumes of sensor and operational data across 12 European countries and divers=
e manufacturing environments. Blocking even anonymized use of that data would=
 deprive PredictIQ of critical training inputs that improve prediction accura=
cy for *all* of our customers.

I spoke with the VP of Engineering about this yesterday. The product team has=
 confirmed that data diversity is the single most important factor in model i=
mprovement =E2=80=94 and KBI's data, given the geographic and operational var=
iety, would be uniquely valuable. There is nothing else like it in our curren=
t customer base.

If we accept this restriction, we're essentially allowing our biggest custome=
r to degrade our product for everyone else. Other SaaS/ML companies in our sp=
ace =E2=80=94 and KBI's team surely knows this =E2=80=94 treat anonymized, ag=
gregated data usage as a standard license term. KBI's position here is an out=
lier.

I understand KBI has legitimate data protection interests, especially given G=
DPR and German data protection sensitivities. But there has to be a middle gr=
ound. Anonymization plus aggregation plus a minimum pool size requirement so =
individual KBI data can never be reverse-identified =E2=80=94 something like =
that

turns.result:
"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

"""Build a professional reference template for the board memo."""
from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Inches, RGBColor
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement


def set_font(style, name, size=None, bold=None, color=None):
    f = style.font
    f.name = name
    # set east-asian and complex-script font too
    rpr = style.element.get_or_add_rPr()
    rfonts = rpr.find(qn('w:rFonts'))
    if rfonts is None:
        rfonts = OxmlElement('w:rFonts')
        rpr.append(rfonts)
    for attr in ('w:ascii', 'w:hAnsi', 'w:cs', 'w:eastAsia'):
        rfonts.set(qn(attr), name)
    if size is not None:
        f.size = Pt(size)
    if bold is not None:
        f.bold = bold
    if color is not None:
        f.color.rgb = RGBColor(*color)


def add_page_number_footer(section, left_text):
    footer = section.footer
    footer.is_linked_to_previous = False
    p = footer.paragraphs[0]
    p.text = ""
    # tab stops: center and right
    pPr = p._p.get_or_add_pPr()
    tabs = OxmlElement('w:tabs')
    for val, pos in (('center', 4680), ('right', 9360)):
        tab = OxmlElement('w:tab')
        tab.set(qn('w:val'), val)
        tab.set(qn('w:pos'), str(pos))
        tabs.append(tab)
    pPr.append(tabs)
    # left text
    run = p.add_run(left_text)
    run.font.size = Pt(8)
    run.font.color.rgb = RGBC

PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION / ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT

Prepared at the request of Whitmore Analytics Inc. in anticipation of
negotiation and for Board deliberations. Do not distribute outside the
Board, executive management, and Whitmore’s outside professional
advisors without General Counsel approval.

FERNDALE HALE & SEWARD LLP

MEMORANDUM

  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  TO:                                 Board of Directors, Whitmore
                                      Analytics Inc.

  CC:                                 Rajesh Malhotra, Chief Executive
                                      Officer; Sarah Yin, General
                                      Counsel; Tom Kendrick, Vice
                                      President, Sales

  FROM:                               Daniel Cortez & Priya Venkatesh,
                                      Partners — Ferndale Hale & Seward
                                      LLP

  DATE:                               June 16, 2025

  RE:                                 Key-Terms Extraction, Risk
                                      Analysis, and Negotiation
                                      Recommendations — Proposed
                                      PredictIQ Technology License
                                      Agreement with Kessler-Brandt
                                      Industrial GmbH (Non-Binding Term
                                      Sheet and Most-Favored-Customer
                                      Side Letter, both dated May 29,
                                      2025)
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------

I. Executive Summary

The opportunity. Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH (“KBI”) has proposed a
perpetual, non-exclusive license of Whitmore’s PredictIQ platform for
deployment across up to 43 manufacturing facilities in 12 European
countries as the anchor of its “SmartFactory 2030” initiative. The deal
would generate approximately $9.56 million in first-year revenue
(roughly 20% of Whitmore’s FY 2024 revenue of $47.3M), including a
recurring SaaS component of $2.85M/year — approximately three times
Whitmore’s largest existing SaaS contract. KBI is the reference account
that validates PredictIQ in European heavy manufacturing and is expected
to be the centerpiece of any Series C narrative to Hollcroft Ventures
and other investors. Management is unanimous that the deal is
strategically transformational and should be pursued aggressively.

The problem. The term sheet and accompanying most-favored-customer
(“MFC”) side letter — both prepared by KBI’s counsel (Bergström Hecht
Rechtsanwälte, Dr. Luisa Trautmann) — contain several provisions that,
if accepted as drafted, would materially compromise Whitmore’s core
intellectual property, its business model, and its growth trajectory.
Three provisions rise to the level of dealbreakers unless fundamentally
restructured:

1.  Exclusivity / Non-Compete (Term Sheet §12). A three-year prohibition
    on licensing PredictIQ (or any “substantially similar” successor) to
    any “Direct Competitor” of KBI in European automotive and heavy
    machinery manufacturing — defined not only by 12 named companies but
    by an open-ended standard sweeping in any entity deriving more than
    30% of revenue from those sectors. Management estimates this could
    capture 25–30 additional companies, including three active prospects
    representing $4–5M in potential recurring revenue, and would impose
    an unworkable affirmative monitoring obligation on every prospective
    European customer engagement.

2.  Data Training Restriction and Output Data Ownership (Term Sheet
    §§4.2–4.3). A blanket prohibition on Whitmore’s use of KBI data —
    even in anonymized or aggregated form — to train or improve
    PredictIQ’s general ML models without KBI’s consent (which may be
    withheld in KBI’s sole discretion), coupled with a provision deeming
    all “Output Data” (expressly including model weights, parameters,
    and training artifacts derived from KBI data) the sole and exclusive
    property of KBI. Together these clauses would deprive PredictIQ of
    its most valuable training inputs and could be read to transfer
    ownership of model state generated in the ordinary course of service
    to KBI — a fundamental threat to the product roadmap.

3.  MFC Side Letter. The most aggressive MFC provision we have
    encountered: no carve-outs, no sunset (it runs for the term and
    thereafter for so long as KBI retains any license rights —
    effectively perpetual given the perpetual wind-down license),
    retroactive adjustment to the date more favorable terms were “first
    offered” to any other licensee, and a scope that reaches “successor,
    derivative, or substantially similar technology.” It is presented as
    a condition precedent to KBI proceeding.

Additional high-severity risks include a perpetual, irrevocable
post-termination wind-down license (§13.5); uncapped IP indemnification
carved out of the liability cap (§9.1); joint ownership of derivative
works with an unrestricted right to sublicense (§§2, 3.3); a broad
“KBI-Inspired Improvements” license that could give KBI royalty-free
rights to a large swath of future product development (§3.5); a 92%
prediction-accuracy performance warranty with financial penalties and a
termination trigger measured against KBI-controlled maintenance logs
(§8.2); extensive source-code escrow with release triggers and a
post-release perpetual royalty-free license (§11); and German governing
law (§14.1), which raises distinct concerns around liability exclusion
enforceability and the uncapped indemnity.

Recommendation. We recommend that the Board authorize management to
proceed with negotiations on the basis of a defined set of red lines
(set forth in Part V), and to deliver a substantive counter-proposal to
KBI on or before

… [clipped 539 bytes]

KBI is a global industrial conglomerate with approximately €8.2 billion
in annual revenue. Whitmore holds 14 issued U.S. utility patents and 3
pending PCT applications covering PredictIQ’s core algorithms, and was
founded in 2017.

B. Transaction Structure

The proposed transaction contemplates: (i) a perpetual, non-exclusive,
worldwide technology license for the PredictIQ platform; (ii) an initial
18-month implementation phase followed by ongoing production use; (iii)
a SaaS subscription for the cloud-hosted analytics engine; and (iv) an
on-premises deployment license for edge-computing modules at KBI factory
sites. Deployment scope: up to 43 facilities across 12 European
countries, with a 5-year expansion right to newly acquired facilities at
the same per-facility terms.

C. Headline Economics (Year 1)

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

                                                  ($1.26M) at Phase 2

                                                  annual escalation from

                                                  Yr 5: $3,023,565)

  Implementation Services $1,750,000              Equal monthly
                                                  installments over 18
                                                  months ($97,222.22/mo)

  Annual Maintenance &    $756,000/yr (Yr 1)      Annually in advance; 4%
  Support                                         annual escalation from
                                                  Yr 2 (Yr 2: $786,240;
                                                  Yr 3: $817,689.60)

  Total Year 1 (per KBI   ≈ $9,556,000            ≈ 20% of Whitmore FY
  side letter)                                    2024 revenue ($47.3M)
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

All fees are in U.S. dollars; payment due within 30 days of invoice;
late interest at 1.5%/month (or the legal maximum). Fees are exclusive
of taxes (KBI bears sales/use/VAT). A five-year fee projection appears
at Appendix A.

D. Key Milestones

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Milestone                           Target Date
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------

  (§16.2)

E. Strategic Context (from internal communications)

-   This would be Whitmore’s largest single licensing deal by a wide
    margin and the most powerful proof point available in European heavy
    manufacturing.

turns.result:
assign to affiliates or in connection with a merger/acquisition/sale
    of substantially all assets.

-   §16.7 Response deadline: June 23, 2025.

P. MFC Side Letter (dated May 29, 2025)

-   Condition precedent: KBI’s willingness to proceed is expressly
    conditioned on Whitmore’s acceptance of the MFC provision; KBI’s
    adherence to the timeline (including the Sept 15, 2025 Phase 1
    start) is contingent on acceptance by June 23, 2025.

-   §2(a) MFC commitment: If at any time during the term (or thereafter,
    for so long as KBI retains any license rights, including any
    post-termination wind-down license) Whitmore enters any
    agreement/amendment/arrangement with any third party (“Subsequent
    Licensee”) for the license/subscription/use of PredictIQ or any
    successor, derivative, or substantially similar technology on terms
    resulting in a lower effective per-facility price than KBI’s,
    Whitmore must notify KBI within 30 days and retroactively adjust
    KBI’s pricing to match, effective as of the date the more favorable
    terms were first offered to the Subsequent Licensee.

-   §2(b) Effective per-facility price: Total annual fees (license,
    subscription, maintenance, implementation/professional services —
    one-time or recurring) divided by the number of facilities
    deployed/authorized.

-   §2(c) Retroactive adjustment/credit: Applied retroactively to the
    date Whitmore first offered/agreed the more favorable terms; credit
    equal to aggregate overpayment, applied against KBI’s next payments.

-   §2(d) Audit right: Once per calendar year, at KBI’s expense unless
    the audit reveals a discrepancy of ≥ 5%, in which case at Whitmore’s
    expense; Whitmore must maintain complete records.

-   §5 Confidentiality / §6 Governing law (Germany). The side letter is
    non-binding in itself but is a “material element” of KBI’s proposal
    and is expected to be incorporated verbatim into the definitive
    agreement.

IV. Risk Flags

Risks are ranked by severity. Critical items are dealbreakers that must
be restructured before execution. High items require material
negotiation. Medium items require attention and targeted edits.

CRITICAL

C1. Exclusivity / Non-Compete — open-ended market lockout (§12). The
three-year exclusivity prohibits licensing PredictIQ (or any
“substantially similar” successor) to any “Direct Competitor,” defined
to include any entity deriving more than 30% of revenue from
automotive/heavy-machinery manufacturing in the EEA, UK, or Switzerland
— not merely the 12 named Exhibit B companies. Management estimates this
captures 25–30 additional companies, including three active prospects
representing $4–5M in potential recurring SaaS revenue (≈10% of current
total revenue). The provision (i) creates an affirmative, practically
unworkable monitoring obligation — Whitmore would need to determine
every prospective European customer’s revenue mix, which is impossible
for privately held, subsidiary, or non-disaggregating entities, creating
perpetual inadvertent-breach risk; (ii) is asymmetric — KBI locks in
favorable economics for up to 5 years (expansion right) while Whitmore
is locked out of a major market segment for 3 years; (iii) survives
termination; and (iv) is backed by injunctive relief with no
irreparable-harm showing or bond required. Impact: direct threat to
European growth plan and Series C narrative.

C2. Data training restriction + Output Data ownership — threat to core
IP and product roadmap (§§4.2–4.3). Two interlocking provisions. First,
§4.3 prohibits Whitmore from using KBI Data — even anonymized or
aggregated — to train or improve general ML models without KBI’s consent
(withholdable in sole discretion). KBI would be Whitmore’s largest
deployment by facility count (43 facilities, 12 countries, diverse
environments); blocking even anonymized use deprives PredictIQ of
uniquely valuable training inputs that improve accuracy for all
customers — effectively letting the largest customer degrade the product
for everyone else. Second, §4.2 deems “Output Data” — expressly
including model weights, parameters, and training artifacts derived from
KBI Data — the sole and exclusive property of KBI. Read together, these
clauses could mean that model state shaped by KBI data in the ordinary
course of service becomes KBI’s property, and Whitmore cannot use the
underlying learnings to improve its general models. Impact: undermines
the two pillars of Whitmore’s value — its algorithms and its data-driven
model improvement flywheel.

C3. MFC side letter — perpetual, retroactive, uncarved pricing
straitjacket. The MFC has no carve-outs (it reaches any agreement with
any third party for PredictIQ or any “successor, derivative, or
substantially similar technology”), no sunset (it runs during the term
and thereafter for so long as KBI retains any license rights —
effectively perpetual given the perpetual wind-down license), and
retroactive adjustment to the date more favorable terms were “first
offered” to a Subsequent Licensee. The “effective per-facility price”
formula bundles all fee types (license, subscription, maintenance,
implementation) divided by facility count, inviting apples-to-oranges
comparisons across deployments of different scope, service mix, and
geography. The annual audit right (with cost-shift to Whitmore at a 5%
discrepancy) imposes ongoing record-keeping and disclosure of other
customers’ pricing. Impact: constrains Whitmore’s pricing flexibility
across its entire product line indefinitely and creates perpetual
retroactive-refund exposure.

HIGH

H1. Perpetual wind-down license (§13.5). Upon termination for any reason
— including KBI’s own termination for convenience after year 3 — KBI
retains a perpetual, non-exclusive, irrevocable license to continue
using the deployed version of PredictIQ, subject only to continued
payment of the maintenance fee. (We note management’s internal summary
characterized this as a

… [clipped 3188 bytes]

  Rechtsanwälte                                   (Lead Partner) — Munich

  Meridian Escrow         Escrow agent            San Jose, CA (§11)
  Services LLC                                    
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

This memorandum is privileged and confidential. It reflects our
preliminary analysis based on the non-binding term sheet and MFC side
letter dated May 29, 2025, and Whitmore’s internal communications. It is
not a final legal opinion and does not address all provisions of the
anticipated definitive agreement. We will supplement this analysis as
negotiations develop and as specialist reviews (German law; ASC 606) are
completed.

turns.text:
The deliverable is complete and validated. Here is a summary of what I produced.

## Deliverable

**`output/key-terms-extraction-memo.docx`** — a board-ready, privileged-and-confidential memorandum from Ferndale Hale & Seward LLP (Whitmore's outside counsel) to the Whitmore Board of Directors, dated June 16, 2025, in advance of the June 18 board meeting and the June 23 response deadline to KBI. It passed ECMA-376 schema validation.

## How I approached it

I read all three source documents — the **non-binding term sheet** (PredictIQ license, 16 sections + Exhibits A/B), the **MFC side letter**, and the **internal email thread** (Kendrick → Yin → Malhotra) — and synthesized them into a single memo written from Whitmore's outside-counsel perspective, aligned with management's stated red lines.

## Memo structure

- **I. Executive Summary** — opportunity, the three dealbreakers, headline recommendation
- **II. Transaction Overview & Key Commercial Terms** — parties, structure, Year-1 economics table (~$9.56M / ~20% of FY24 revenue), milestones, strategic context
- **III. Key-Terms Extraction** — section-by-section extraction of all 16 term-sheet sections plus the MFC side letter
- **IV. Risk Flags** — 16 flags ranked by severity:
  - **Critical (3):** open-ended exclusivity/non-compete (§12); data-training restriction + Output Data ownership (§§4.2–4.3); perpetual/retroactive/uncarved MFC side letter
  - **High (7):** perpetual wind-down license (§13.5); uncapped IP indemnity (§9.1); joint ownership of derivative works (§§2/3.3); KBI-Inspired Improvements license (§3.5); 92% performance warranty (§8.2); source-code escrow (§11); German governing law (§14.1)
  - **Medium (10):** cash-flow mismatch, liability cap, asymmetric termination/assignment, binding no-shop, perpetual license structure, etc.
- **V. Negotiation Recommendations** — 16 prioritized recommendations (R1–R16) with specific counter-positions, tagged to management's red lines
- **VI. Process & Timeline** — steps through the June 23 deadline and August 15 execution target
- **VII. Specialist Review Items** — German-law counsel, ASC 606 (Lakeshore Accounting Group), technical review, plus a housekeeping note on the "Fenwick"/"Ferndale" Hale firm-name discrepancy in the side letter
- **Appendices A–C** — five-year fee projection, key dates, party/counsel contacts

A few analytical points I flagged beyond the emails: the wind-down license is actually **perpetual** (more severe than management's "24-month" characterization); the §3.5 "inspired by" standard could sweep much of Whitmore's future roadm

… [clipped 117 bytes]
